topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the office was the loudest thing I had ever heard. Dust motes danced in the slivers of afternoon sun cutting across the mahogany desk where Elias’s awards used to sit. I kept running a hand over the smooth, cool surface, a nervous habit. I hadn't seen him for three days, and the usual low thrum of anticipation—the need to catch his eye, to hear his measured advice—was just a dull ache behind my ribs. When I finally pushed open the door, expecting the familiar scent of aged paper and strong coffee, the space was sterile. A small, neatly typed note was affixed to the doorframe, a final, impersonal declaration of departure. I stood there, my shoulders slumping inward, the air suddenly too thin to draw in.

[story 2]

My feet felt heavy, dragging across the polished floor as I walked into the studio. The usual vibrant chaos of the workspace—the scattered sketches, the humming machinery—seemed muted, drained of its energy. I stopped just inside the doorway, my gaze snagging on the empty chair at the main console. A hollow sensation settled in my chest, a tightness that made it hard to take a full breath. I searched the room, half-expecting to find him fiddling with a tool or leaning against the workbench, but there was only an unnerving orderliness. The absence of his presence felt like a physical void, pressing down on my chest until my throat felt constricted.

[story 3]

I reread the email for the tenth time, the words blurring slightly on the screen. It was brief, professional, utterly devoid of warmth. I stared at the clock on the wall, watching the seconds crawl by with agonizing slowness. A strange, cold detachment washed over me, making my hands tremble slightly as I gripped the printout. I felt a profound sense of deflation, as if a vital support structure had simply dissolved. I didn't move to leave; instead, I sank onto the nearest stool, letting my head drop forward, the weight of the news settling deep into my core, making the room spin just a little.
